summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers fundamentally misunderstand how their careers work because educational institutions fail to teach business realities. The core thesis is that engineers are hired to create business value—increasing revenue or reducing costs—not to write code, and they should position themselves accordingly. He covers salary negotiation, networking, the irrelevance of academic credentials, the overvaluation of startup equity, and the critical importance of communication skills. The post systematically dismantles engineer-culture shibboleths like technical stack identity, modesty, and the belief that merit speaks for itself. McKenzie contends that framing yourself as a business-value creator rather than a programmer is the single most important career move an engineer can make.
key_points: 90% of programming jobs involve boring line-of-business software, and engineers should embrace that software's value is measured by revenue generated or costs reduced, not technical sophistication
Calling yourself a 'programmer' makes you sound like an expensive cost center ripe for outsourcing; instead, describe yourself by the business outcomes you've delivered
Most desirable jobs are filled through personal networks before they're ever posted publicly, making relationship-building more valuable than resume optimization
Communication skills and the ability to frame your work in business terms matter more than raw programming ability for career advancement
Startup equity is almost always worth far less than engineers believe due to dilution, liquidation preferences, and high failure rates
money_quotes: If you call yourself a programmer, someone is already working on a way to get you fired.
It does not matter to the company that the reporting form is the world's simplest CRUD app, it only matters that it either saves the company costs or generates additional revenue.
You really want to be attached to Profit Centers because it will bring you higher wages, more respect, and greater opportunities for everything of value to you.
Nobody ever outsources Profit Centers. Attempting to do so would be the setup for MBA humor.
'What is your previous salary?' is employer-speak for 'Please give me reasons to pay you less money.' Answer appropriately.
You radically overestimate the average skill of the competition because of the crowd you hang around with.
Information about the position travels at approximately the speed of beer, sometimes lubricated by email.
Modesty is not a career-enhancing character trait.
key_insight: Engineers are not hired to write code—they are hired to create business value, and the most successful ones learn to describe themselves and their work in those terms rather than in technical jargon.
